mnemonics.title: PEP 8 Naming Rules
mnemonics.content: Remember: 'snake_case for variables/functions, UPPERCASE for constants, CamelCase for classes'. Think of a Python naming zoo: snakes on the ground (lowercase), giraffes standing tall (CamelCase), and elephants with big letters (constants).

mnemonics.title: Keyword Chant
mnemonics.content: Chant this phrase to remember common Python keywords: 'And Or Not In Is For While Def Class Try Except Finally With As Import From Return Yield Lambda Pass Break Continue Elif Raise Global Nonlocal'. First letters form AONIFWDC TEFWARLCBCEG - it's a chant, not a perfect acronym!

mnemonics.title: Literal LINGO
mnemonics.content: Remember LINGO for literal types: Literals Include Numeric (int, float, complex), Boolean, None, String/Char. Each literal is an actual value written directly in your code.

mnemonics.title: Identifier Rules - ALNUM
mnemonics.content: ALNUM: Alphabets, Numbers allowed (but not at start), Letters (A-Z, a-z), Underscore (_), Memory case-sensitive. Identifiers can only start with letter or underscore, then use letters, digits, underscores.

acronyms.usage: Follow PEP 8 style guide for naming identifiers in Python code

acronyms.expansion: Special Names, Names with _ underscore, Each character allowed, Alpha first, Keywords forbidden
acronyms.usage: Quick recall for identifier naming rules: must start with letter/underscore, case-sensitive, cannot be keyword
Three special literal types in Python beyond basic integer and boolean

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a conference name tag - it uniquely identifies a person. An identifier is a 'name tag' that uniquely identifies a variable, function, or class in memory. The name tag (identifier) must follow rules: readable, starts with letter/underscore.

Picture a theater with 'reserved' signs on certain seats - these seats (keywords) are permanently reserved and no one else (identifier) can use them. Once a seat is reserved, it's never available for general naming.

Think of filling out a form: when you write your exact age (25), name ('John'), or answer (True/False) directly on the paper - these are literals. They are the actual data written directly in your code, not placeholders.

Visualize a storage room with labeled boxes. The box label is the identifier, and inside is the actual object/value. You use the label (identifier) to find and access what's inside (the object in memory).

analogies.analogy: Like naming a pet - the name must start with a letter (can't start with number), can include letters and numbers after first character, no special characters or spaces allowed. 'my_dog' is valid, '1dog' or 'my-dog' is not.

Like reserved words in English grammar - words like 'the', 'and', 'or' have fixed meanings and cannot be used as variable names. In Python, 'if', 'else', 'for', 'while' are reserved and cannot become identifier names.

Like different types of concrete values in a form: exact numbers (age: 21), decimal numbers (height: 5.9), text answers (name: 'Raj'), true/false answers (married: False). Each literal type represents a different kind of fixed value you can write directly.
Like 'John' and 'john' being treated as different people - Python treats 'Score', 'score', and 'SCORE' as three completely different identifiers. Always be consistent with your casing.
